scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle wrote the Politics in 4th-c. BCE Athens, using its legal framework and contemporary Greek city-state norms as his primary reference.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit in the mid-4th century BCE (-348), as he describes these legal mechanisms as active features of contemporary Greek life.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle wrote the Politics in 4th-c. BCE Athens; the classification of democratic and constitutional norms reflects his contemporary observations.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348); the practice of providing pay for poor jurors was a staple of 4th-century Athenian democracy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle analyzes the universal principles of Greek city-state constitutions from his perspective in 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle identifies this as a universal constitutional principle active during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes variations in property qualifications based on the contemporary Greek constitutional theories prevalent in his time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle discusses these qualifications as contemporary political realities during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly discusses the constitution and educational system (agoge) of Sparta.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Although the Spartan agoge was ancient, Aristotle describes it as a currently operating system at the time of his writing (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly refers to the Spartan syssitia (common messes) and their dress codes.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes the Spartan common meals as a contemporary cultural and legal institution at the time of his writing (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the specific electoral rights of the Spartan people regarding the Elders and Ephors.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes the Spartan electoral process as an active political mechanism during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle defines tyranny and the rights of free men based on contemporary 4th-century Greek political philosophy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle's definitions of 'free men' and 'tyranny' are contemporary reflections of 4th-century BCE political norms (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle observes the stability of constitutions based on the role of the middle class in his contemporary Greek world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This assessment of the middle class's political share was recorded by Aristotle during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes specific oligarchic artifices as part of his general contemporary political analysis.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle discusses legal mechanisms for magistracies as they existing in various contemporary Greek city-states.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This legal distinction was observed and documented by Aristotle during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly cites the laws of Charondas, a lawgiver for Catana and other Chalcidian cities, though the observation is contemporary.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle cites Charondas' laws as an example within his current political discourse, dated to his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's description of weapon laws refers to the contemporary practices of Greek poleis analyzed in his work.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The exclusion of the poor from heavy weaponry is treated as a contemporary legislative fact by Aristotle in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle details the compulsory nature of physical training for elites as a feature of contemporary Greek constitutions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly refers to democratic devices, such as the pay for service characteristic of the Athenian democracy of his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle identifies basic social protections required to prevent unrest in contemporary Greek city-states.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule regarding protection from insult is a contemporary social observation made during Aristotle's floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes universal participation in the deliberative body as the hallmark of contemporary Greek popular governments.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The right of all citizens—including the low-born—to deliberate was a contemporary reality in 4th-century democracies (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle contrasts the legislative habits of contemporary oligarchies and democracies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Fines to compel jury service are described as current political tools by Aristotle during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The provision of payment for poor attendees is a specific feature of the Athenian democracy contemporary to Aristotle.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle defines aristocratic constitutional forms based on their control of war and auditing functions as practiced in the Greek world.
